Emphasized anātman (no inherent self-identity), conditioned change, and achieving peace in this life rather than focusing on an eternal afterlife. ​

The Buddha lived after the Persian conquest of Gandhāra and Sindh (ca. ​ 518–517 BC) and before Alexander’s invasion (330–325 BC), reacting to cultural and religious shifts introduced by Zoroastrianism. ​

column1: Gaumata the Magus was a Mede who led a rebellion against Darius I, aiming to restore the worship of daivas (gods) suppressed by Zoroastrianism. ​ This rebellion highlighted the conflict between Zoroastrian monotheism and older polytheistic traditions. ​
Gaumata demolished temples, likely Zoroastrian fire temples or those dedicated to Ahura Mazda, which Darius later restored. ​ These events are recorded in the Behistun Inscription. ​
Relationship and Evidence Related to the Buddha:

The Buddha rejected Zoroastrian concepts such as a perfect, all-powerful God (Ahura Mazda), the eternal soul, and absolute distinctions between good and evil. ​ Early Buddhist texts, such as the Pali Canon, emphasize anātman (no-self) and conditioned change, opposing Zoroastrian beliefs. ​

The Buddha’s teachings emphasized anātman (rejection of inherent self-identity), conditioned change (impermanence and interconnectedness), and the Middle Path (avoiding extremes, including absolutist ideas).

The Buddha lived after the Persian conquest of Gandhāra and Sindh (ca. ​ 518–517 BC) and before Alexander’s invasion (330–325 BC). ​ Archaeological evidence from Gandhāra shows Persian influence, including inscriptions and administrative practices. ​
The epithet Śākamuni (‘Sage of the Scythians’) connects the Buddha to Scythian and Central Eurasian cultural influences, suggesting he may have been a Scythian (Saka) by origin. ​

Burial practices in Taxila, such as exposing the dead to vultures, reflect Zoroastrian influence in Gandhāra during the Buddha’s time. ​ These customs align with Zoroastrian practices described by Herodotus and Aristobulus. ​

The Buddha’s rejection of Zoroastrian ideas aligns with cultural changes brought about by the Achaemenid Empire’s conquest of the region, countering the absolutist-perfectionist beliefs introduced by Zoroastrianism. ​

column2: Lao Tan (老聃) is reconstructed as Old Chinese *Gotama ~ *Gautama, aligning with the Buddha’s personal name.
Lao (老) is equated with k’ao (考) in ancient Chinese texts, showing phonetic and semantic similarity. ​
The name Lao Tan was adapted in Chinese to mean ‘Old Long-ears,’ reflecting transcriptional adjustments. ​
Tao (道) is reconstructed as Old Chinese *daʁwa ~ *daʁma, showing phonetic similarity to Old Indic dharma. ​
Tao’s meaning as ‘path, way’ aligns with the Buddhist concept of Dharma as the ‘way’ or ‘truth.’ ​
Śramaṇa is consistently used in foreign sources (Greek, Chinese, Persian, etc.) ​ to mean ‘Buddhist practitioner.’ ​
Early Chinese transcriptions of Śramaṇa include 沙門 (shāmén), preserving the phonetic structure of the original term. ​
Early Taoist texts, such as the Chuangtzu and Tao Te Ching, reflect Buddhist ideas like anātman and conditioned change. ​
The text on the jade finial describing breath-control yoga aligns with early Buddhist practices, suggesting transmission of ideas.
